demographics: I am 24 years old. I am female. I am of another race. I have never been married. I completed 4 years of college (bachelor's degree). My family income is $75,000 to $89,999. I am a strong Democrat. I am liberal. I have no religious affiliation. I never attend religious services.

events.event_text: I joined the school's Black Student Union last year, and it's been a transformative experience. I've met so many incredible people who share my experiences, and together we're advocating for change within our predominantly white institution.

Last year, I was pulled over by the police for no apparent reason. I felt humiliated andtargeted, especially when I saw the officer's skeptical look after hearing my name. This experience only strengthened my conviction that systemic change is needed to eliminate racial biases in our institutions.

When I was 16, I went to the local police station to ask about a community outreach program I'd heard about. The officer I spoke to blatantly ignored my questions and assumed I was there for something else, ignoring my polite manners and treating me like a criminal just because of how I looked. It was a wake-up call to the bias I'd face growing up black in this town.

When I was 18, I was one of the few black students selected for a student exchange program to Japan. It was eye-opening to experience a culture where my skin color didn't brand me as a outsider. The locals were incredibly welcoming, and I met students from all over the world. It made me realize how much systemic racism impacts my daily life back home and fueled my passion for social justice.

At 19, I became the first in my family to attend a predominantly white college. Experiencing culture shock and racial microaggressions was tough, but it also sparked a deeper understanding of systemic racism, fueling my activism.

At 21, I was stop-and-frisked by police while walking home from my evening shifts at the local library. Despite showing my student ID and explaining that I live just around the corner, they detained me for 30 minutes, making me feel humiliated and targeted. This experience renewed my determination to fight against racial profiling.

At 22, I organized a campus protest against a local policy that targeted students of color. Police showed up, escalating the situation. I was tackled and arrested, spending a night in jail. Despite the intimidation, this experience fueled my activism and brought more attention to our cause.
[Step 8 skipped: constraint could not be satisfied — ]

compressed_summaries.summary: This person, active in their school's Black Student Union, advocates for change within a predominantly white institution. They have experienced racial profiling by police, feeling humiliated and targeted, which strengthened their conviction for systemic change. Despite facing bias, they were selected for a student exchange program to Japan, where they experienced acceptance and realized the impact of systemic racism at home.

This person, at 19, navigated culture shock and racial microaggressions as the first in their family to attend a predominantly white college, sparking activism against systemic racism. At 21, they were humiliated by a 30-minute stop-and-frisk detention, renewing their fight against racial profiling. At 22, they organized a campus protest, was arrested, and spent a night in jail, further fueling their activism and drawing attention to the cause.

persona_card: This 24-year-old, college-educated, liberal Democrat of color, unaffiliated with any religion, is deeply committed to dismantling systemic racism, having personally experienced profiling and bias. She advocates for significant economic redistribution, trusts few institutions, and holds strong structuralist views on racial inequality. Despite her progressive economic and racial attitudes, she maintains a slight moral traditionalism. She is welcoming and open towards social outgroups, having experienced acceptance abroad and witnessed microaggressions at home.
